messages.text:
Research the official Claude Code skill format/structure. I need to know:

1. What is the required directory structure for a Claude Code skill?
2. What files are required (SKILL.md, etc.)?
3. What is the expected format of SKILL.md (frontmatter, sections)?
4. Are there any conventions for how skills should organize their code, scripts, assets?
5. How are skills installed/discovered by Claude Code?

Search the web for "Claude Code custom skill format", "Claude Code SKILL.md", "Claude Code slash command custom skill" and check the official Anthropic documentation.

Also check if there are any local files that define the skill format - look in the user's home directory for any Claude Code configuration or documentation:
- ~/.claude/
- Any SKILL.md files in ~/.claude/

Be thorough - I need the official specification.
I'll research the official Claude Code skill format and structure for you.
Great! Now let me read the actual SKILL.md file from your local skills directory and check the Anthropic repository for official specifications.
Let me try to get the official documentation through a different approach.
Now let me fetch some of the real-world examples to understand the format better.
Let me check the GitHub repository of awesome-claude-skills to get real examples and documentation.
Perfect! Now let me get a few specific examples from the GitHub repositories to show real-world patterns.

Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile the official specification for you.

## Official Claude Code Skill Format and Structure

Based on official Anthropic documentation and your local skill implementation, here's the complete specification:

### 1. Required Directory Structure

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md (REQUIRED)
├── scripts/ (optional)
├── references/ (optional)
├── assets/ (optional)
└── agents/ (optional)
```

### 2. Required Files

**SKILL.md** is the ONLY required file. It contains:
- YAML frontmatter with metadata (at the top, between `---` markers)
- Markdown instructions that Claude follows when the skill is invoked

### 3. SKILL.md Format Specification

**Structure:**
```markdown
---
name: skill-name-in-lowercase
description: Clear, specific description (200 characters max) of when Claude should use this skill
---

# Skill Title

Detailed markdown instructions for Claude to follow...
```

**Required Frontmatter Fields:**
- `name`: Lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens only. Becomes the `/slash-command`
- `description`: Critical metadata. Claude uses this to decide when to automatically load your skill. Must be specific enough for Claude to select the right skill from 100+ possibilities (200 characters max)

**Optional Frontmatter Fields:**
- Version information
- Allowed tools/permissions
- Other configuration metadata

### 4. Supporting Directory Purposes

**`scripts/`** (optional)
- Executable code for deterministic/repetitive tasks
- Python scripts, shell scripts, or other runnable code
- Claude executes these for complex multi-step operations, data transformations, API interactions
- Referenced from SKILL.md when needed

**`references/`** (optional)
- Documentation and knowledge bases
- Detailed guides, API documentation, configuration specs
- Claude loads these into context as needed when SKILL.md references them
- Keep implementation details here to avoid cluttering the main instructions

**`assets/`** (optional)
- Files used in output: templates, icons, fonts, example outputs
- Subdirectories like `templates/` for template files
- Language variants like `assets/templates/en/`, `assets/templates/zh-cn/`

**`agents/`** (optional)
- Configuration files for sub-agents (e.g., `openai.yaml`)
- Used for multi-agent skill orchestration

### 5. Skill Installation and Discovery

**Installation Locations:**

| Location | Scope | Auto-discovered |
|----------|-------|-----------------|
| `~/.claude/skills/` | Global (all projects) | Yes |
| `.claude/skills/` | Project-specific | Yes |
| Nested `.claude/skills/` | Subdirectory-scoped | Yes (v2.1.6+) |

**Discovery Mechanism:**
- Claude automatically discovers skills in `.claude/skills/` directories
- Live change detection allows editing skills during sessions without restarting
- Nested skills discovery finds skills in subdirectory `.claude/skills/` when working with files in those subdirectories

### 6. Best Practices from Official Guidelines

**Description Quality:**
- The description is critical—Claude uses it to choose the right skill from potentially 100+ available
- Provide enough detail so Claude knows when to select this skill
- Rest of SKILL.md provides implementation details

**File Organization:**
- Keep SKILL.md focused on high-level instructions
- Move complex details to `references/` files
- Reference bundled resources from SKILL.md so Claude knows what they contain and when to load them

**Supporting Resources:**
- Only include resources that Claude will actually need
- Use references for documentation, scripts for deterministic operations
- Example: Your memorytree-workflow skill uses 10+ reference files for configuration details

### 7. Real-World Example Structure

Your `memorytree-workflow` skill at **C:\Users\ai\.claude\skills\memorytree-workflow** demonstrates best practices:

```
memorytree-workflow/
├── SKILL.md (frontmatter + instructions)
├── scripts/
│   ├── init-memorytree.py
│   ├── upgrade-memorytree.py
│   ├── discover-transcripts.py
│   ├── import-transcripts.py
│   ├── detect-memorytree-locale.py
│   └── utility modules
├── references/
│   ├── project-detection.md
│   ├── memory-layout.md
│   ├── git-policy.md
│   ├── locale-selection.md
│   └── 6 more documentation files
├── assets/templates/
│   ├── en/ (goal.md, todo.md, chat-log.md, agents.md)
│   └── zh-cn/ (translations)
└── agents/
    └── openai.yaml
```

### 8. How Skills Are Used by Claude

1. Claude reads the `description` field to decide whether to use your skill
2. When invoked (via `/slash-command` or automatically), Claude loads the full SKILL.md
3. Claude reads referenced `references/` files as needed for detailed context
4. Claude executes `scripts/` when deterministic operations are required
5. Claude fills in or uses `assets/` templates for output

---

**Key Takeaway:** SKILL.md is the gateway. Its frontmatter determines discovery, and its instructions determine execution. Everything else is supporting detail that gets loaded on demand.

Sources:
- [Extend Claude with skills - Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills)
- [How to create custom Skills - Claude Help Center](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/12512198-how-to-create-custom-skills)
- [Anthropic Skills Repository](https://github.com/anthropics/skills)
- [Skill authoring best practices - Claude API Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/best-practices)
- [Use Skills in Claude - Claude Help Center](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/12512180-use-skills-in-claude)

Based on the search results, here's the official documentation on the Claude Code custom skill format SKILL.md:

## SKILL.md Format Overview

Every skill needs a SKILL.md file with two parts: YAML frontmatter (between --- markers) that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with instructions Claude follows when the skill is invoked.

## Required Structure

The SKILL.md contains the main instructions and is required. This file must start with a YAML frontmatter to hold name and description fields, which are required metadata.

## YAML Frontmatter Fields

The essential frontmatter includes:
- The name field becomes the /slash-command, and the description helps Claude decide when to load it automatically.
- The description is critical for skill selection: Claude uses it to choose the right Skill from potentially 100+ available Skills. Your description must provide enough detail for Claude to know when to select this Skill, while the rest of SKILL.md provides the implementation details.

## Supporting Files

Other files are optional and let you build more powerful skills: templates for Claude to fill in, example outputs showing the expected format, scripts Claude can execute, or detailed reference documentation. Reference these files from your SKILL.md so Claude knows what they contain and when to load them.

skill-name/ ├── SKILL.md (required) │ ├── YAML frontmatter (name, description required) │ └── Markdown instructions └── Bundled Resources (optional) ├── scripts/ - Executable code for deterministic/repetitive tasks ├── references/ - Docs loaded into context as needed └── assets/ - Files used in output (templates, icons, fonts)

For the complete official documentation, visit the [Claude Code Skills documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills).


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

1→---
     2→name: memorytree-workflow
     3→description: Detect, initialize, and maintain a reusable MemoryTree project-memory workspace for any code repository. On activation, immediately detect and report the repo's MemoryTree state.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# MemoryTree
     7→
     8→Use this skill for repo-level project memory. Do not treat it as an application runtime feature unless the user explicitly asks to productize it.
     9→
    10→## On Activation
    11→
    12→When this skill is invoked, immediately perform these steps without waiting for further user input:
    13→
    14→1. Detect the repo state (`not-installed`, `partial`, or `installed`) using `references/project-detection.md`.
    15→2. If `not-installed`: run `scripts/init-memorytree.py` to scaffold the workspace, then report what was created.
    16→3. If `partial`: run `scripts/upgrade-memorytree.py` to add missing pieces, then report what was added.
    17→4. If `installed`: read the active goal, bound todo, and latest chat log. Report the current state (goal summary, todo progress, pending tasks) and ask the user what they want to work on next.
    18→
    19→Do not stop after detection to ask whether you should proceed. Act on the result immediately.
    20→
    21→## Core Behavior
    22→
    23→1. Detect the repo state before changing files. Use `references/project-detection.md`.
    24→2. If MemoryTree is missing, initialize it with `scripts/init-memorytree.py`. If the script cannot run, create the files from `assets/templates/`.
    25→3. If MemoryTree is partial, upgrade it with `scripts/upgrade-memorytree.py` before manual maintenance.
    26→4. If MemoryTree is already installed, maintain only the active goal, bound todo, and current chat log. Use `references/update-rules.md`.
    27→5. Keep the installation compatible with the host repo's branch, PR, CI, and review rules. Use `references/git-policy.md`.
    28→6. Prefer safe defaults: do not add reply suffix requirements unless the host repository already requires them, do not mix code-and-memory commits by default, and do not override branch policy. MemoryTree-only git automation is allowed only inside the isolated flow in `references/git-policy.md`.
    29→7. When locale is not explicit, choose it from repo language first, then system language. Use `references/locale-selection.md`.
    30→8. Keep reply language aligned with user language first, then repo locale. Use `references/response-language.md`.
    31→9. For partial repos, use the non-destructive upgrade path before manual maintenance. Use `references/upgrade-path.md`.
    32→10. When transcript archival is enabled, import transcripts with deterministic code, preserve raw transcripts as evidence in both the current repo mirror and the global archive, generate cleaned Markdown for indexing, keep cross-project backfills and ambiguous ownership cases in the global archive only, and ask once per repository whether raw transcript files may be committed or pushed. Use `references/transcript-archive.md`.
    33→11. When scripts are needed, prefer an existing `uv run python` environment first, then a system `python`, and only fall back to manual scaffolding or conservative manual review if no runner is available. Use `references/execution-environment.md`.
    34→
    35→## Read Order
    36→
    37→1. Repo `AGENTS.md`, if present.
    38→2. Latest file in `Memory/01_goals/`.
    39→3. Latest todo bound to that goal in `Memory/02_todos/`.
    40→4. `Memory/04_knowledge/` only when needed.
    41→5. `Memory/06_transcripts/clean/`, transcript manifests, and repo-local raw transcript mirrors only when transcript history or cross-project memory search is relevant.
    42→6. `Memory/03_chat_logs/` and `Memory/05_archive/` only when needed.
    43→
    44→## Detect
    45→
    46→1. Check whether `Memory/` exists and matches the layout in `references/memory-layout.md`.
    47→2. Classify the repo as `not-installed`, `partial`, or `installed`.
    48→3. If the repo already has stronger rules than the generated templates, preserve the repo rules and only add missing MemoryTree pieces.
    49→
    50→## Upgrade
    51→
    52→1. Use `scripts/upgrade-memorytree.py` for repositories that already have `AGENTS.md`, custom policy files, or partial `Memory/` content.
    53→2. Preserve existing repo policy files by default.
    54→3. Create only the missing MemoryTree pieces.
    55→4. Read the upgrade result and treat `agents_merge_required=true` as a manual review task, not an overwrite instruction.
    56→5. If no Python runner is available, do not create a project-local environment automatically. Fall back to conservative manual inspection and only make low-risk changes unless the user explicitly approved environment setup.
    57→
    58→## Initialize
    59→
    60→1. Run `scripts/init-memorytree.py --root <repo>` and pass `--project-name`, `--goal-summary`, and `--locale` when the user already provided them.
    61→2. Use fresh init only when the repo does not already have `AGENTS.md` or another repo policy source such as a policy-bearing `CONTRIBUTING.md`, PR templates, `CODEOWNERS`, or commitlint config. If repo policy already exists, switch to `scripts/upgrade-memorytree.py` instead of trying to skip `AGENTS.md`.
    62→3. Review the generated `AGENTS.md` before keeping it. Merge it with the repo's existing policy if needed.
    63→4. Keep the initial installation minimal: `Memory/01_goals`, `02_todos`, `03_chat_logs`, optional `04_knowledge`, `05_archive`, and `06_transcripts` only when transcript archival is enabled for the repo or needed for transcript import.
    64→5. Use the locale-specific template files in `assets/templates/<locale>/` as the source of truth for first-run scaffolding.
    65→6. Prefer `--locale auto` when the user did not request a language explicitly.
    66→7. Use `scripts/detect-memorytree-locale.py` when you need a deterministic locale check without scaffolding files.
    67→8. Prefer `uv run python` first, then a system `python`. If neither is available, create first-run files directly from `assets/templates/` instead of creating a project-local virtual environment by default.
    68→9. If transcript archival is enabled for the repo, use `scripts/import-transcripts.py` for one explicit source file and `scripts/discover-transcripts.py` when scanning local client stores. Matching transcripts must update both the repo mirror and the global archive, while unrelated projects, ambiguous ownership cases, or mismatched explicit sources are archived in the global archive only. Let the scripts generate cleaned transcripts deterministically without spending model tokens on the cleaning step.
    69→10. Ask once whether raw transcript files may also be committed to the repository. Record the answer in `AGENTS.md`, keep the files in the repo either way, and exclude `raw` files from automatic staging until the user says yes.
    70→
    71→## Maintain
    72→
    73→1. Change the active goal only after explicit user confirmation of a scope or requirement change.
    74→2. Update the active todo when progress changes, milestones move, or the next task becomes clearer.
    75→3. Append chat logs only. Never rewrite or delete prior entries.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Claude Code skills directory structure, format, and components:

## Skills Directory Structure

Every Skill is defined in a markdown file named SKILL.md (case-insensitive) with optional bundled files that's stored under /scripts, /references, and /assets.

The standard structure for a skill is:

skill-name.zip containing SKILL.md at the root level, along with optional references/, scripts/, assets/, and templates/ directories

Only SKILL.md is required, though the optional directories provide additional capabilities.

## Core Components

**SKILL.md File:**
SKILL.md is a markdown file that follows a two-part structure - frontmatter and content, where the frontmatter configures HOW the skill runs (permissions, model, metadata), while the markdown content tells Claude WHAT to do.

**Supporting Files:**
Optional files let you build more powerful skills: templates for Claude to fill in, example outputs showing the expected format, scripts Claude can execute, or detailed reference documentation.

## Directory Purposes

- **scripts/**: Use scripts/ for complex multi-step operations, data transformations, API interactions, or any task requiring precise logic better expressed in code than natural language.

- **references/**: Put details in references/ etc. for documentation and knowledge bases.

- **assets/**: These bundled files can be Python scripts, shell scripts, font definitions, templates, etc.

- **templates/**: Used for storing templates that Claude can fill in during skill execution.

## Frontmatter Format

Frontmatter includes YAML configuration with fields like name, description, allowed-tools, and version.

Based on the search results, here's the official format for Claude Code skills using SKILL.md:

## Official SKILL.md Format

Every skill needs a SKILL.md file with two parts: YAML frontmatter (between --- markers) that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with instructions Claude follows when the skill is invoked.

### Required Frontmatter Fields

The file must start with a YAML frontmatter to hold name and description fields, which are required metadata.

**Example structure:**

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: Clear description of what the skill does and when to use it.
---
# Skill Title

Detailed instructions and guidelines...
```

### Field Specifications

- **`name:`** Must use lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only.

- **`description:`** Critical—Claude uses this to determine when to invoke your Skill (200 characters maximum).

### Optional Supporting Files

Other files are optional and let you build more powerful skills: templates for Claude to fill in, example outputs showing the expected format, scripts Claude can execute, or detailed reference documentation. Reference these files from your SKILL.md so Claude knows what they contain and when to load them.

The standard skill structure includes optional directories for `scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/` alongside SKILL.md.

Based on the search results, here's the official format for Claude Code skills using SKILL.md:

## Official SKILL.md Format

Every skill needs a SKILL.md file with two parts: YAML frontmatter (between --- markers) that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with instructions Claude follows when the skill is invoked.

### Required Frontmatter Fields

The file must start with a YAML frontmatter to hold name and description fields, which are required metadata.

**Example structure:**

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: Clear description of what the skill does and when to use it.
---
# Skill Title

Detailed instructions and guidelines...
```

### Field Specifications

- **`name:`** Must use lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only.

- **`description:`** Critical—Claude uses this to determine when to invoke your Skill (200 characters maximum).

### Optional Supporting Files

Other files are optional and let you build more powerful skills: templates for Claude to fill in, example outputs showing the expected format, scripts Claude can execute, or detailed reference documentation. Reference these files from your SKILL.md so Claude knows what they contain and when to load them.

The standard skill structure includes optional directories for `scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/` alongside SKILL.md.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Claude Code skill installation and discovery in the `~/.claude/skills` directory:

## Directory Location and Structure

Personal skills for Claude Code should be added to `~/.claude/skills/`, while project-specific skills go in `.claude/skills/`.

## Automatic Discovery

Skills defined in `.claude/skills/` within directories are loaded automatically and picked up by live change detection, so you can edit them during a session without restarting. Additionally, nested skills discovery in Claude Code automatically finds skills in subdirectory `.claude/skills` folders when working with files, introduced in v2.1.6.

## Skill Structure

Custom Skills are created as directories with SKILL.md files that Claude discovers and uses automatically. Every skill needs a SKILL.md file with two parts: YAML frontmatter (between --- markers) that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with instructions Claude follows when the skill is invoked.

## Installation Methods

For Claude Code, skills can be added to `~/.claude/skills/` (personal) or `.claude/skills/` (project) by cloning the GitHub repository and copying the skill folder to your chosen directory.

## Nested Skills Discovery

When working with files in a subdirectory, Claude automatically discovers and loads skills from that subdirectory's `.claude/skills` folder, with skills in nested directories discovered based on your current file context.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the anthropics/skills GitHub repository structure and real examples:

## SKILL.md Structure

Skills are simple to create - just a folder with a SKILL.md file containing YAML frontmatter and instructions. Each skill is self-contained in its own folder with a SKILL.md file containing the instructions and metadata that Claude uses.

The basic folder structure looks like:

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md (required)
│   ├── YAML frontmatter (name, description required)
│   └── Markdown instructions
└── Bundled Resources (optional)
    ├── scripts/ - Executable code for deterministic/repetitive tasks
    ├── references/ - Docs loaded into context as needed
    └── assets/ - Files used in output (templates, icons, fonts)
```


## Real Examples

The repository contains diverse skills:

1. **Algorithmic Art**: Creates gallery-quality computational art that lives and breathes using algorithmic philosophy as the foundation, with seeded randomness for reproducibility.

2. **Frontend Design**: Used when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI).

3. **PDF Processing**: Use when the user wants to do anything with PDF files, including reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs.

4. **Document Co-authoring**: Guides users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation; used when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content, helping users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers.

The repository contains skills that demonstrate what's possible with Claude's skills system, ranging from creative applications (art, music, design) to technical tasks (testing web apps, MCP server generation) to enterprise workflows (communications, branding, etc.).

Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile the official specification for you.

## Official Claude Code Skill Format and Structure

Based on official Anthropic documentation and your local skill implementation, here's the complete specification:

### 1. Required Directory Structure

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md (REQUIRED)
├── scripts/ (optional)
├── references/ (optional)
├── assets/ (optional)
└── agents/ (optional)
```

### 2. Required Files

**SKILL.md** is the ONLY required file. It contains:
- YAML frontmatter with metadata (at the top, between `---` markers)
- Markdown instructions that Claude follows when the skill is invoked

### 3. SKILL.md Format Specification

**Structure:**
```markdown
---
name: skill-name-in-lowercase
description: Clear, specific description (200 characters max) of when Claude should use this skill
---

# Skill Title

Detailed markdown instructions for Claude to follow...
```

**Required Frontmatter Fields:**
- `name`: Lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens only. Becomes the `/slash-command`
- `description`: Critical metadata. Claude uses this to decide when to automatically load your skill. Must be specific enough for Claude to select the right skill from 100+ possibilities (200 characters max)

**Optional Frontmatter Fields:**
- Version information
- Allowed tools/permissions
- Other configuration metadata

### 4. Supporting Directory Purposes

**`scripts/`** (optional)
- Executable code for deterministic/repetitive tasks
- Python scripts, shell scripts, or other runnable code
- Claude executes these for complex multi-step operations, data transformations, API interactions
- Referenced from SKILL.md when needed

**`references/`** (optional)
- Documentation and knowledge bases
- Detailed guides, API documentation, configuration specs
- Claude loads these into context as needed when SKILL.md references them
- Keep implementation details here to avoid cluttering the main instructions

**`assets/`** (optional)
- Files used in output: templates, icons, fonts, example outputs
- Subdirectories like `templates/` for template files
- Language variants like `assets/templates/en/`, `assets/templates/zh-cn/`

**`agents/`** (optional)
- Configuration files for sub-agents (e.g., `openai.yaml`)
- Used for multi-agent skill orchestration

### 5. Skill Installation and Discovery

**Installation Locations:**

| Location | Scope | Auto-discovered |
|----------|-------|-----------------|
| `~/.claude/skills/` | Global (all projects) | Yes |
| `.claude/skills/` | Project-specific | Yes |
| Nested `.claude/skills/` | Subdirectory-scoped | Yes (v2.1.6+) |

**Discovery Mechanism:**
- Claude automatically discovers skills in `.claude/skills/` directories
- Live change detection allows editing skills during sessions without restarting
- Nested skills discovery finds skills in subdirectory `.claude/skills/` when working with files in those subdirectories

### 6. Best Practices from Official Guidelines

**Description Quality:**
- The description is critical—Claude uses it to choose the right skill from potentially 100+ available
- Provide enough detail so Claude knows when to select this skill
- Rest of SKILL.md provides implementation details

**File Organization:**
- Keep SKILL.md focused on high-level instructions
- Move complex details to `references/` files
- Reference bundled resources from SKILL.md so Claude knows what they contain and when to load them

**Supporting Resources:**
- Only include resources that Claude will actually need
- Use references for documentation, scripts for deterministic operations
- Example: Your memorytree-workflow skill uses 10+ reference files for configuration details

### 7. Real-World Example Structure

Your `memorytree-workflow` skill at **C:\Users\ai\.claude\skills\memorytree-workflow** demonstrates best practices:

```
memorytree-workflow/
├── SKILL.md (frontmatter + instructions)
├── scripts/
│   ├── init-memorytree.py
│   ├── upgrade-memorytree.py
│   ├── discover-transcripts.py
│   ├── import-transcripts.py
│   ├── detect-memorytree-locale.py
│   └── utility modules
├── references/
│   ├── project-detection.md
│   ├── memory-layout.md
│   ├── git-policy.md
│   ├── locale-selection.md
│   └── 6 more documentation files
├── assets/templates/
│   ├── en/ (goal.md, todo.md, chat-log.md, agents.md)
│   └── zh-cn/ (translations)
└── agents/
    └── openai.yaml
```

### 8. How Skills Are Used by Claude

1. Claude reads the `description` field to decide whether to use your skill
2. When invoked (via `/slash-command` or automatically), Claude loads the full SKILL.md
3. Claude reads referenced `references/` files as needed for detailed context
4. Claude executes `scripts/` when deterministic operations are required
5. Claude fills in or uses `assets/` templates for output

---

**Key Takeaway:** SKILL.md is the gateway. Its frontmatter determines discovery, and its instructions determine execution. Everything else is supporting detail that gets loaded on demand.

Sources:
- [Extend Claude with skills - Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills)
- [How to create custom Skills - Claude Help Center](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/12512198-how-to-create-custom-skills)
- [Anthropic Skills Repository](https://github.com/anthropics/skills)
- [Skill authoring best practices - Claude API Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/best-practices)
- [Use Skills in Claude - Claude Help Center](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/12512180-use-skills-in-claude)
